title: Treasury: No boost to growth from budget
full_text:
Treasury says there will be no overall boost to economic growth and productivity from the Albanese government's most significant changes to the tax system in a quarter of a century.
The revelation confirms that the contentious tax changes to negative gearing, capital gains and trusts are more about equity and redistributing wealth than about growing the economic pie.
The admission came as Reserve Bank of Australia governor Michele Bullock said the housing tax changes, in combination with the RBA's three interest rates rises, were slowing the housing market.
More broadly, Bullock said she was concerned about the economy's weak productivity results that would be felt by Australians in their pay packers.
"You can't get any growth in real incomes if you don't have productivity growth, but that's not something we can control," she told a Senate hearing on Thursday.
Treasury's economic assessment of the budget contrasts with Treasurer Jim Chalmers' budget speech, in which he said the budget was "making the economy more productive to lift living standards over time".
On Thursday as the government's controversial tax bill passed the House of Representatives, Chalmers once again defended the budget measures.
"The reforms in this bill are about reducing distortions in the market that have been encouraging people to invest in established houses rather than in other assets like units or shares," he said.
"The current 50 per cent discount is arbitrary. It undercompensates some investors for inflation and overcompensates others."
Investors and business have argued that replacing the 50 per cent discount on capital gains with an inflation indexation model would deter investment in high-risk, high-return ventures.
Treasury secretary Jenny Wilkinson said some of the budget's targeted tax measures for innovators and small business would help productivity.
Younger people who primarily earned their income from wages would be better off from the $250 Working Australians Tax Offset, and the top 1 per cent of earners would be most affected by the changes to capital gains, trusts and negative gearing, she said.
But senior Treasury officials told a Senate hearing on Thursday the overall package had not changed the department's medium-term forecasts for growth and productivity.
Treasury assistant secretary for macroeconomics Angelia Grant said, "There isn't a shift in the medium-term parameters as a result of the tax package."
Bullock said the impact of the government's budget changes would not make much difference to aggregate economic demand.
Productivity has stagnated since the COVID-19 pandemic and edged up by just 0.3 per cent over the year to March 31, according to new figures published on Wednesday.
Another measure of living standards, real economic growth per person, has been negative for 10 of 15 quarters under the Albanese government, said Kevin You, a senior fellow at the conservative Institute of Public Affairs think tank. The Productivity Commission estimates that productivity growth has historically been responsible for 80 per cent of national income growth in Australia over the past 30 years.
Chalmers hosted a three-day economic roundtable last August in an effort to identify measures to boost productivity.
Grant said the government's efforts to reduce red tape for businesses, accelerate approvals, and promote international trade would help productivity.
In addition, the budget's tax changes gave Treasury greater confidence that productivity growth could return to the medium-term assumption of 1.2 per cent a year, which economists have described as optimistic.
The Reserve Bank of Australia has lowered its productivity growth forecast to 0.7 per cent over the next two years.
In the May 12 budget, the government axed negative gearing except for existing landlords and newly built homes, and imposed a minimum 30 per cent tax on discretionary trust distributions and real capital gains, including from property, shares, crypto and other assets. Wilkinson earlier sidestepped questions about whether Treasury had done detailed modelling on the economic growth and productivity impacts from the budget's tax package.
Wilkinson said some of the measures would help businesses' productivity and innovation.
She highlighted a $3.5 billion tax support package for business investment, including reintroducing and expanding loss refundability for businesses, expanding venture capital incentives, better targeting the research and development tax incentive, and making permanent the $20,000 instant asset write-off for small business.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: John Kehoe | Economics
heading: Banking and Economy
